title: AWS Integration
description: Manage real S3 resources with idempotent handlers, sliced from the site-publisher example.
provider_intro: The site-publisher shares one <0>S3Client</0> through context. The provider builds the client, registers <1>onCleanup</1> to destroy it, and hands the connection to every descendant:
provider_notice: Any component under the provider calls <0>useAws</0> to get the client and region. One connection serves the whole tree, and it is destroyed exactly once when the tree <1>disposes</1>.
ensure_bucket_intro: The bucket handler creates the bucket and treats <0>BucketAlreadyOwnedByYou</0> as success, because handlers re-run on every startup:
ensure_bucket_notice: Catching the already-owned error is the idempotency pattern for cloud resources — the handler converges on the desired state instead of assuming a blank slate. Any other error still <0>throws</0> and fails the node.
website_config_intro: After the bucket exists, the same handler enables website hosting and publishes the site URL with <0>setOutputs</0>:
website_config_notice: The URL becomes a reactive output — <0>bucket.url()</0> — that child components and effects can consume the moment it is set.
changed_check_intro: Each uploaded file is its own resource. Before uploading, the handler compares the file's content hash against the persisted outputs via <0>setOutputs(prev => …)</0>:
changed_check_notice: When the hash matches, the handler returns early — <0>prev</0> is the last persisted value, so an unchanged file costs zero API calls on redeploy.
upload_intro: Only changed files reach the <0>PutObjectCommand</0>. The new hash is persisted with the path, ready for the next run's comparison:
warning_idempotent: Cloud handlers must be idempotent: they re-run on every startup, so create-or-confirm patterns and change detection are what keep repeated runs from duplicating work or failing on existing resources.
see_walkthrough: See the full deployment story, manifest hashing included, in the <0>site-publisher walkthrough</0>.
